title: Reading Query Results
summary: Learn how to interpret and understand the results of SQL queries.

sketchBlocks.title: Understanding SQL Query Results
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
When you run a SQL query, the results are displayed in a table format. Each column in the result corresponds to a column in the database table, and each row represents a record that matches the query criteria. 

For example, if you run the query `SELECT name, grade FROM students;`, you will see a table with two columns: `name` and `grade`. Each row will show the name and grade of a student from the `students` table.

```sql
SELECT name, grade FROM students;
```

Try it yourself: Write a query to select the `name` and `city` columns from the `students` table and run it to see the results.

sketchBlocks.title: Interpreting Query Results
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
The results of a SQL query can tell you a lot about the data in your database. For instance, if you see multiple rows with the same `name` but different `grades`, it might indicate that there are multiple students with the same name but different performance levels.

Consider the query:

```sql
SELECT name, grade, city FROM students;
```

This query will return all students' names, grades, and cities. By examining the results, you can identify patterns or anomalies in the data.

Your turn: Modify the query to include the `age` column and observe how the additional data changes your understanding of the students' information.

quizDraft.title: Select All Students
quizDraft.prompt: Write a SQL query to select all columns from the students table.
quizDraft.hint: Use the asterisk (*) to select all columns.

help.concept: The asterisk (*) is used to select all columns in a SQL query.
help.hint_1: The asterisk (*) is a wildcard for all columns.
help.hint_2: Use * to select all columns without listing them individually.

quizDraft.title: Select Specific Columns
quizDraft.prompt: Write a SQL query to select the `name` and `age` columns from the students table.
quizDraft.hint: List the columns you want to select after the SELECT keyword.

help.concept: Specify the columns you want to retrieve in the SELECT statement.
help.hint_1: Separate column names with a comma.
help.hint_2: Ensure you use the correct column names.

quizDraft.title: Select Students by City
quizDraft.prompt: Write a SQL query to select the `name` and `city` columns from the students table.
quizDraft.hint: Use the SELECT statement to specify the columns.

help.concept: The SELECT statement retrieves specified columns from a table.
help.hint_1: Ensure you list the correct columns after SELECT.
help.hint_2: Separate column names with a comma.
